title: Anti-Spoofing & Liveness Detection
concept: Liveness detection prevents spoofing attacks (like holding up a photo or video of a user) by verifying the face is a real 3D human.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary purpose of Triplet Loss?

options.text: To pull embeddings of the same identity together and push different identities apart.
To crop faces from images using bounding boxes.
To classify facial expressions into emotions.

exercises.explain: Correct! Triplet Loss coordinates distances in embedding space to optimize similarity metrics.

cards.title: The printed photo trick
cards.body: Imagine you set up a face unlock system. A coworker prints a high-resolution photo of your face from social media and holds it up to the camera. If your model only analyzes standard 2D pixels, it will see your face and unlock. To be secure, the system must detect if the target is alive.

cards.title: What is Anti-Spoofing?
cards.body: Anti-Spoofing (Liveness Detection) is a security mechanism that determines whether a captured face is a real, live human or a fake representation (a printout, a video replay, or a 3D mask). Techniques include active checks (asking users to blink or turn their head) and passive checks (analyzing depth or skin textures).

cards.title: A flat canvas vs. a statue
cards.body: Think of liveness detection like checking if a shape is a painting or a sculpture. A 2D photo has no depth — it's flat like a canvas. A real human face has a 3D structure, with the nose closer to the lens than the ears.

cards.title: Active and Passive checks
cards.body: Apple FaceID uses passive liveness detection by projecting over 30,000 invisible infrared dots onto your face to build a precise 3D depth map. Other apps use active checks: they prompt the user to blink, smile, or repeat a random sequence of numbers.

cards.title: Video replays aren't liveness
cards.body: You might think a video of a user playing on an iPad screen would bypass face verification because the eyes are moving. However, modern sensors detect the flat screen's light reflections, screen pixels, and lack of depth, identifying it as a spoof.

cards.title: Liveness Verification
cards.body: Comparing a 2D spoof attempt to a real human check:

left.text:
Zero depth variation
Flat surface reflections
Access: Denied

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of liveness detection in facial verification?

options.text: To verify that the face belongs to a real, live person rather than a photo or screen
To identify the user's name in a public database
To increase the contrast of the camera stream
To compress the face embedding vector size

exercises.explain: Liveness detection distinguishes real human presence from static photos or video replays.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Asking a user to blink or nod their head is an example of passive liveness detection.
exercises.explain: False. Prompting the user to perform an action (blinking, nodding) is an active liveness check.

exercises.sentence: Projecting infrared dots to build a 3D mesh of a face checks for ___ depth cues.
exercises.explain: Checking depth curves checks the spatial coordinates of facial features.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a passive anti-spoofing technique?

options.text: Analyzing the texture and micro-reflections of the skin surface from a single static frame
Displaying a popup asking the user to rotate their neck left and right
Searching the face crop against a database of 100,000 users

exercises.explain: Analyzing skin textures and light reflections from a single frame is passive because the user does not need to perform any action.

exercises.prompt: You are building a secure ATM face validation gate. How should you design the liveness detection subsystem to prevent hackers from bypass-attacks using tablet displays?

options.text: Combine an active challenge (e.g. ask the user to blink) with a depth sensor to verify a 3D human structure.
Simply match the 2D crop vector; if similarity is 0.99, trust it.
Lower the matching threshold to 0.1 so fake faces match easily.
Require users to hold a printed photo of themselves next to their face.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Combining active challenges with physical depth sensors ensures the system is resilient against flat-screen replays and printed photos. Fantastic work! Next: assembling the entire verification pipeline.
